story:
Dr. Lynxala, a Patheurian physicist running the Alliance's most secret lab, Blackdeep, double-checks numbers that could birth a wormhole—or a disaster. The stakes: gram-scale antimatter caged in a generator, a particle accelerator primed nearby, and the ever-present fear of turning the experiment chamber into a small sun or, worse, a black hole. His team, brilliant and rumpled, works beneath the glow of a tacky neon ad: a reclining green-outlined vixen on a Vulane beach purring, "Visit Vulane! …I think it's time for a break." They get a blip in the gateway—then a fizzle. It's the first real sign of life in months, and corks of Vulanian wine pop like applause.

Lynxala returns to his spacious, lonely quarters on the airless moon and does what he always does when jealousy of his colleagues' messy romances flares: he writes. Love and Wormholes: Interspecies Dialogues in Deep Space is the work of "Professor Skywine," his secret pseudonym. He's a genius in physics and a fool in everything from spelling to cybersecurity. His literary agent on Vulane—sweet voice, beach selfies, lots of flattery—has sent "feedback." He wanted someone pretty and incurious, a soft landing for his ego, not a prybar into his life.

He downloads her notes to a private server to keep them off Blackdeep's secured mainframe. Hidden inside, a script slips through an unsecured entry point and uses the one system no one bothered to lock down: the coffee machines, which the faceless bosses tied to the mainframe to speed up caffeine but didn't secure because who would hack a coffee maker? When Lynxala—already in star-pattern pajamas—orders, "Cream, two sugars," the panel chirps a brief error. In that blink, the vixen who wrote the virus guesses his passcode—"Skywine"—and takes administrator control. Lynxala, soothed by honeyed praise that seems to catch every metaphor he barely intended, drifts to sleep. Across the base, colleagues slump under Vulanian wine while a dish swivels and fires a fat data packet toward a supposedly empty patch of sky "near" the Sh'ra homeworld, the perfect decoy if anyone checks the logs.

Morning brings routine: eggs, spreadsheets, and a request from his exhausted team for a day off. He grants it—until a green message from the unseen bosses snaps back: No breaks; proceed. He loads Protocol 3-0-1-0—the exact recipe he'd uploaded. But the virus has quietly nudged key ratios and retargeted the accelerator. Warmed above spec, it lances a torrent of atoms straight into the antiproton trap. Matter and antimatter meet; in a quadrillionth of a second, mass turns to pure energy. Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared, indeed. Sirens don't have time to finish their first wail.

Six months later, a woman with synthetic eyes, hiding in a blank patch of space near Sh'ra territory, catches the transmitted haul: a century of wormhole work in a single sip. She was the "agent," the vixen in the glow, the saboteur who rode a coffee machine into a government mainframe. "Time for a break," she murmurs, and sets a course home.

Geniuses shine in narrow beams. It was enough to spark a gateway. It was also enough to miss the neon warning posing as a joke.
